title: Blurring: Smoothing an Image
concept: Blurring replaces each pixel with the average of its neighbors, smoothing out sharp transitions to reduce noise and fine detail.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is an image filter (kernel)?

options.text: A small grid of numbers that slides across an image, recalculating each pixel from its neighbors
A tool that renames image files
A way to compress an image into a smaller size

exercises.explain: Right! A filter is a sliding window that recomputes pixels from their local neighborhood — today we'll use that to blur.

cards.title: Portrait mode's soft background
cards.body: Ever notice how a portrait photo has a crisp face but a dreamy, soft background? That soft look comes from blurring — deliberately smoothing pixels together so fine detail disappears.

cards.title: What is blurring?
cards.body: Blurring (or smoothing) is a filter that replaces each pixel with the average of itself and its nearby neighbors. Averaging pulls sudden jumps in brightness toward the middle, so sharp transitions soften and small details fade.

cards.title: Mixing wet paint together
cards.body: Imagine dabbing wet paint colors right next to each other and smearing them together with a finger. The hard boundary between colors disappears into a smooth gradient. Averaging nearby pixels does the same thing to a photo.

cards.title: Cleaning up a noisy photo
cards.body: A photo taken in low light often has random speckles of noise — pixels that don't match their surroundings. Sliding an averaging window across it blends each speckle into its neighbors, so the noise fades and the image looks cleaner.

cards.title: Blurring isn't random smudging
cards.body: It looks like a smudge, but nothing random is happening. Every pixel's new value comes from the exact same rule — average the neighborhood — applied consistently across the whole image. A bigger averaging window blurs more; a smaller one blurs less.

cards.title: Small window vs. large window
cards.body: How window size changes the blur:

left.text:
Averages just a few neighbors
Light smoothing, detail mostly kept

right.text:
Averages many neighbors
Heavy smoothing, fine detail lost

exercises.prompt: What does a blurring filter do to each pixel?

options.text: Replaces it with the average of itself and its nearby neighbors
Deletes it and leaves a gap
Sorts neighboring pixels from dark to light

exercises.explain: Correct — averaging neighboring pixels is exactly how blurring smooths an image.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A larger averaging window produces a stronger blur.
exercises.explain: True. More neighbors get mixed into each average, so detail and noise smooth away more heavily.

exercises.sentence: Blurring reduces noise by replacing each pixel with the ___ of its neighborhood.
exercises.explain: Average is correct — that's the core operation behind every blur filter.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows a blur filter being useful?

options.text: Smoothing out grainy speckles in a photo taken in dim light
Making the edges of a logo look crisper
Converting a color photo to black and white

exercises.explain: Reducing graininess by averaging neighboring pixels is exactly what blurring is for.

exercises.prompt: A security camera photo is full of grainy noise from low light, but you don't want to lose the overall shapes in the scene. What's the tradeoff of applying a strong blur filter?

options.text: It reduces the noise, but a window too large will also wash away real detail like edges and small objects
It has no downside — blurring only ever removes noise and never affects real detail
It sharpens edges while removing noise
It compresses the file without changing any pixel values

exercises.explain: Exactly — blurring trades detail for smoothness. Next: what if we want the opposite effect instead?
